title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Viability of Nuclear Power in a Green Future

texts.A: [37]Nuclear energy remains the only genuinely scalable, low-carbon solution currently available to meaningfully address our increasingly urgent climate crisis.[/37] [39]The inherently intermittent nature of wind and solar generation means they fundamentally cannot provide the reliable, constant 'baseload' power that modern industrial society requires continuously, day and night, regardless of weather conditions.[/39] [40]While public fear of catastrophic nuclear accidents is understandably high, the actual statistical safety record of nuclear power is objectively superior to almost every other major energy source, including the chronic air pollution generated by fossil fuels, which kills millions annually.[/40] The real obstacle to progress isn't the technology, waste management, or economics—it is the political cowardice of governments unwilling to make long-term commitments to developing the next generation of small modular reactors. These innovative designs could reliably and cleanly power entire cities for centuries to come. We are sacrificing our sustainable energy future for short-term electoral comfort and misplaced public anxiety.
texts.B: To describe nuclear power as genuinely 'green' or meaningfully 'clean' is to conveniently ignore the profoundly toxic environmental legacy of uranium mining, the contamination of indigenous lands during extraction, and the as-yet completely unresolved global problem of permanent radioactive waste storage. [40]We are essentially creating a geological time bomb, irresponsibly bequeathing future generations an enormous burden they never consented to carry and may ultimately prove unable to manage safely.[/40] [38]The massive upfront capital costs and multi-decade construction timelines of large nuclear plants mean they are simply too slow and too expensive to help us meet our critical near-term climate goals.[/38] [37]We should instead redirect those enormous sums into large-scale, coordinated investments in utility-scale battery storage and intelligent smart grids, which can make renewable energy sources as reliably available as any traditional baseload power plant.[/37] The future of energy is distributed, genuinely clean, and decentralised. Nuclear belongs firmly in the past.
texts.C: The politically constructed binary choice between 'nuclear' and 'renewables' is a damaging oversimplification that distracts from the genuine complexity of a responsible energy transition strategy. A thoughtfully diversified and technically resilient national energy portfolio remains the only truly responsible approach to ensuring long-term energy security and economic stability. I agree that constructing large-scale traditional nuclear reactors is no longer economically justifiable in most contemporary market conditions. However, [39]I share the view that the intermittency and baseload reliability problem associated with wind and solar generation represents a genuine and technically significant engineering challenge that cannot simply be wished away.[/39] Nuclear power could serve as a secondary, grid-stabilizing reserve force in future electricity systems, but only if the international community can establish a credible and transparent multilateral governance framework for managing the resulting radioactive waste through genuine long-term cooperation. We need evidence-based pragmatism and flexibility, not rigid ideological positions, to navigate this complex challenge successfully.
texts.D: The economic argument against further significant investment in nuclear power has now become essentially insurmountable for any honest analyst, regardless of ideological inclination. [38]As the levelized cost of solar panels, wind turbines, and associated grid-scale storage technologies continues to fall dramatically year after year, nuclear power becomes an increasingly unjustifiable financial black hole for taxpayers and energy consumers alike.[/38] I share the genuine environmental skepticism regarding the long-term integrity of deep geological nuclear waste repositories, but my primary and overriding concern remains the existential risk of weapons proliferation—the more countries that develop civilian nuclear energy programs, the exponentially more difficult it becomes to prevent the eventual spread of weapons-grade fissile material to hostile actors. History provides clear warnings that we cannot afford to ignore. We must move decisively and urgently toward a fully decentralized, 100% renewable energy system that is simultaneously safer, economically superior, and more democratically governed than any nuclear alternative.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the reliability of a grid powered solely by renewables?
questions.explanation: Expert A believes renewables are too intermittent for industrial baseload. Expert B argues that battery storage and smart grids can make renewables just as reliable.

questions.question: Which expert shares D's concern about the financial costs associated with nuclear energy?
questions.explanation: Expert D calls nuclear a 'financial black hole.' Expert B also highlights the 'massive capital costs' and long construction times that make it impractical.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to C regarding the necessity of a stable power supply (baseload)?
questions.explanation: Expert C agrees that the 'baseload problem' of renewables is real. Expert A makes this the central point of their argument for nuclear energy.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the ethical implications of using nuclear technology?
questions.explanation: Expert B focuses on the 'toxic legacy' and 'ticking time bomb' for future generations. Expert A, however, argues that nuclear is the only 'pragmatic response' to the climate crisis, implying a different moral priority.
